---
title: "Analysis examples"
author: "PRdSR"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: moon
    slide-number: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    transition: fade
    progress: true
    chalkboard: true
    highlight-style: github
    footer: "Analysis examples | PRdSR"
editor: visual
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Analysis examples/essentials

Quarto enables you to weave together content and executable code into a finished presentation. To learn more about Quarto presentations see <https://quarto.org/docs/presentations/>.

Here I'll add some examples/essentials for analysis in R.

# PCA

Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is used to:

\- Reduce dimensionality

\- Explore structure in data

\- Identify patterns (e.g. batch effects, groups)

## Load the library

```{r}
library(stats)
```

## Example data {.smaller .scrollable}

```{r}
data("iris")

# Create extra metadata
set.seed(123)

iris$Group <- sample(c("Case", "Control"), nrow(iris), replace = TRUE)
iris$Batch <- sample(c("Batch1", "Batch2", "Batch3"), nrow(iris), replace = TRUE)
iris$Age <- round(rnorm(nrow(iris), mean = 70, sd = 10))

str(iris)
```

# Perform PCA using prcomp {.smaller}

```{r}
# PCA needs only numeric columns, so we subset those
pca_input <- iris[, c("Sepal.Length", "Sepal.Width", "Petal.Length", "Petal.Width")]

pca_result <- prcomp(pca_input, scale. = TRUE)

summary(pca_result)
```

# Visualize the PCA

## Load visualisation packages

```{r}
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
```

## Add metadata back to the PCA results {.smaller}

```{r}
pca_df <- as.data.frame(pca_result$x)

# Add metadata back
pca_df$Species <- iris$Species
pca_df$Group <- iris$Group
pca_df$Batch <- iris$Batch
pca_df$Age <- iris$Age
```

::: callout-important
Metadata must match the same samples used in the PCA. If these don’t match after filtering or NA handling, the colours will break.
:::

```{r}
nrow(pca_input)
nrow(pca_df)
nrow(iris)
```

# Example: Effect of missing values on sample alignment {.smaller}

```{r}
# Introduce some missing values
pca_input_na <- pca_input
pca_input_na[1:10, 1] <- NA

# Remove rows with NA
clean_data <- na.omit(pca_input_na)

nrow(pca_input_na)
nrow(clean_data)
```

::: callout-note
If rows are removed from the PCA input, the metadata must be filtered in the same way before colouring the PCA plot.
:::

# Create a PCA plot

```{r}
ggplot(pca_df, aes(PC1, PC2, colour = Species)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "PCA plot coloured by Species",
    x = "PC1",
    y = "PC2"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

# Create PCA plots for different covariates {.smaller}

```{r}
p1 <- ggplot(pca_df, aes(PC1, PC2, colour = Group)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  labs(title = "Group", x = "PC1", y = "PC2") +
  theme_minimal()

p2 <- ggplot(pca_df, aes(PC1, PC2, colour = Batch)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  labs(title = "Batch", x = "PC1", y = "PC2") +
  theme_minimal()

p3 <- ggplot(pca_df, aes(PC1, PC2, colour = Species)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  labs(title = "Species", x = "PC1", y = "PC2") +
  theme_minimal()

p4 <- ggplot(pca_df, aes(PC1, PC2, colour = Age)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  labs(title = "Age", x = "PC1", y = "PC2") +
  theme_minimal()

p1 + p2 + p3 + p4
```

# Scree plot

## Prepare data for visualisation {.smaller}

```{r}
scree_pca_input <- data.frame(
  Component = 1:length(pca_result$sdev),
  Variance = pca_result$sdev^2 / sum(pca_result$sdev^2)
)

scree_pca_input
```

## Create the scree plot

```{r}
ggplot(scree_pca_input, aes(x = Component, y = Variance)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "steelblue") +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "Scree plot",
    x = "Principal Components",
    y = "Proportion of Variance Explained"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

# Key takeaways

:::: callout-tip
::: incremental
-   PCA uses numeric data only
-   Missing values must be handled before PCA
-   Metadata must match the same samples used in PCA
-   Start simple, then build complexity
:::
::::

# Optional extras

## PCA plot (scores) using plotly

<https://plotly.com/ggplot2/pca-visualization/>

```{r}
library(plotly)
library(ggfortify)

p <- autoplot(pca_result, data = iris, colour = "Species")

ggplotly(p)
```

## PCA plot (loadings) using plotly

```{r}
p <- autoplot(pca_result, data = iris, colour = "Species", loadings = TRUE)

ggplotly(p)
```

# Setting up the environment

When loading `pca_input` there are several options depending on the type of file you are loading, for example `.csv`, `.xlsx`, or `.tsv`.

## here

One package that can be very useful when setting up an analysis is the `here` package (<https://here.r-lib.org/>). This package enables easy file referencing.

## Installation

```{r}
# install.packages("here")
```

## Loading library

```{r}
library(here)
```

## Usage

```{r}
here::i_am("analysis_examples.qmd")

here()
```
